_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Anson County, NC. Record survival and courthouse history for one of NC's oldest counties — formed 1750 from Bladen.
display_name: Anson County, North Carolina — Courthouse Atlas

previous_courthouses.location: Various early locations in what was then an enormous county covering most of south-central NC
previous_courthouses.fate: Anson's original territory was so large that county court was held at multiple locations. Early record-keeping was minimal.

previous_courthouses.location: Wadesboro — successive frame and brick courthouses
previous_courthouses.fate: Replaced over time as population grew and records accumulated. No catastrophic fire documented.

records_destroyed: Some early court records and tax lists may never have been properly maintained
record_losses.description: Anson County's earliest records (1750s-1770s) reflect frontier conditions — record-keeping was sporadic, courts met irregularly, and the enormous county territory made consistent documentation difficult. This is not record loss from a catastrophic event but rather the natural thinness of colonial frontier records. Once the county stabilized and its territory was reduced through division, record quality improved significantly.
record_losses.narrative_impact: Anson County's colonial records are thin but they exist — making it one of the few counties in this cluster with records reaching back to mid-colonial North Carolina. Gaps in {ancestor_name}'s record trail before 1780 likely reflect frontier informality, not destruction.
record_losses.source: NC State Archives. Guide to County Records.

deeds.completeness: Fair for earliest decades, good from 1780 onward. One of the longest deed series in south-central NC.
deeds.gaps: Early entries sparse. Continuous from approximately 1770.
deeds.repository: Anson County Register of Deeds, Wadesboro; NC State Archives microfilm
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Ancestry.com NC land records

wills_estates.completeness: Fair from 1750s, improving through the late 1700s.
wills_estates.gaps: Colonial-era loose papers may be incomplete.
wills_estates.repository: Anson County Clerk of Court; NC State Archives

marriages.gaps: Pre-1780 marriages documented only in court minutes or not at all.
marriages.repository: Anson County Register of Deeds; NC State Archives

court_minutes.completeness: Good from 1771. The court minutes provide rich detail on colonial and early republic life in the Pee Dee corridor.
court_minutes.gaps: Pre-1771 minutes may not survive or may have been destroyed.
court_minutes.repository: NC State Archives; Anson County Clerk of Court

vital_records.repository: NC Office of Vital Records; NC State Archives

substitute_sources.source_type: Colonial court records and land grants
substitute_sources.description: Anson County's colonial court records document land disputes, apprenticeships, road orders, and estate divisions from the 1760s-1770s. Colonial land grants from the NC Secretary of State extend even earlier.
substitute_sources.repository: NC State Archives; published in Colonial Records of North Carolina (Saunders, ed.)
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Colonial records name relationships, occupations, and neighbors. Essential for pre-Revolutionary families in the Pee Dee corridor.
substitute_sources.source: Saunders, William L., ed. Colonial Records of North Carolina. Raleigh: State of NC, 1886-1890.

substitute_sources.description: Anson County was formed from Bladen County in 1750. Records before that date are in Bladen County.
substitute_sources.repository: Bladen County Register of Deeds, Elizabethtown; NC State Archives
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: For the earliest settlers in the Pee Dee corridor, Bladen County records and colonial land grant records are the starting point.
substitute_sources.source: NC State Archives. Guide to County Records.

substitute_sources.description: Anson County was enumerated in every federal census from the first in 1790. Census records provide continuous documentation from 1790 onward.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for tracking families. Anson's large enslaved population before 1865 means slave schedules (1850, 1860) are critical for Black family research.
substitute_sources.source: National Archives. Federal Census Schedules, Anson County, NC.

narrative_hooks: Anson County's records reach back to 1750 — one of the longest paper trails in south-central North Carolina — which means {ancestor_name}'s family may be documented from the colonial era if they were among the Pee Dee corridor's earliest settlers.

The original Anson County encompassed half of south-central NC — if {ancestor_name} lived anywhere in this region before 1780, the records may start in Wadesboro even if the modern county is Richmond, Montgomery, or Stanly.

Anson's colonial court minutes from the 1770s are among the richest in south-central NC — recording land disputes, apprenticeships, and the daily business of a frontier county where {ancestor_name}'s earliest ancestors may appear by name.

Before Anson County existed, this territory was Bladen County — if {ancestor_name} was here before 1750, the trail leads to Elizabethtown and the colonial records that predate every county in this cluster.
source_references: Saunders, William L., ed. Colonial Records of North Carolina. 10 vols. Raleigh: State of North Carolina, 1886-1890.
NC State Archives. Guide to County Records in the North Carolina State Archives. Raleigh: Division of Archives and History, 2001.
Leary, Helen F. M., ed. North Carolina Research: Genealogy and Local History. 2nd ed. Raleigh: North Carolina Genealogical Society, 1996.
Powell, William S. The North Carolina Gazetteer. 2nd ed. Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 2010.
